summary: Piper Harron reflects on her unconventional mathematics PhD thesis, which she describes as initially intended as a 'grenade' against academia but evolved into something more constructive. She candidly discusses spending 10 years on her dissertation while battling imposter syndrome, feeling behind her peers, and processing the systemic pressures of being a Black woman in mathematics. Her awakening to feminism and anti-racism, catalyzed by Trayvon Martin's death, led her to rewrite her thesis as something accessible to non-mathematicians. She hopes it will show that research math isn't alien and help graduate students feel less alone in their struggles.
key_points: Harron entered Princeton with only 7 math classes compared to peers' 20-30, creating persistent feelings of inadequacy
Her brain processes mathematics differently than most mathematicians—she needs complete logical understanding rather than hand-waving explanations
Stress from graduate school literally hospitalized her, leading her to consciously 'give up stress' and leave without graduating in 2009
The killing of Trayvon Martin and subsequent acquittal awakened her to conscious anti-racism and prompted her to restart her thesis entirely
She deliberately wrote her thesis to be accessible to non-mathematicians and struggling graduate students, not for established mathematicians
money_quotes: TEN YEARS?! WHAT KIND OF PERSON SPENDS TEN YEARS ON ONE DOCUMENT AND STILL HAS TO BUILD UP COURAGE TO DISCUSS IT OPENLY?
Please tell me the rules I must abide by in order to make no waves!
I can't just understand the forest, I also need the trees, and the leaves, and the space between, and I need to be able to go forwards and backwards logically between it all.
The way people talk to you when you don't understand what they're saying. It's its own terrible language and I had to listen to it for years.
I realized stress had sent me to the hospital and I was thoroughly against that on principle, so I gave up stress.
A black teenaged boy was shot dead for no reason and his assailant was found not guilty because black boys are scary.
I am thrilled at the idea that mathematicians might read and enjoy my thesis, but I didn't write it for them.
key_insight: Academia's rigid structures and unspoken rules can hospitalize you, but reclaiming your work as something accessible and human—rather than performing for gatekeepers—transforms it from a weapon of escape into something that might actually help others.
